retrieved_docs.title: Schistosomiasis -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, elimination efforts would likely need to be multifactorial involving education and mass treatment programs. Unfortunately, it would also require significant cost and time investments by endemic countries, many of which are poverty-stricken.

retrieved_docs.title: Schistosomiasis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the pathophysiology of the trematode Schistosoma haematobium and risk factors for infection. Summarize the physical exam findings associated with Schistosomiasis (Schistosoma haematobium). Identify the common chronic complications of patients with Schistosomiasis (Schistosoma haematobium). Outline the importance of collaboration and communication amongst the interdisciplinary team in order to treat and identify infected and at-risk individuals in order to enhance the delivery of care and improve outcomes for patients living in endemic countries affected by Schistosomiasis (Schistosoma haematobium). Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Schistosomiasis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most prevalent form of urogenital schistosomiasis is chronic. While acute schistosomiasis (or Katayama fever) can be caused by S. haematobium, it is more recognized in other schistosome species.  In endemic areas, the average age of initial infection is age 2, with an increasing worm burden for the next 10 years. Infection intensity and prevalence in a region of Kenya were highest in children aged 5 to 14 years old. Infection prevalence and intensity are possibly due to the frequency of contact with water during daily activities, as adult individuals with high water contact during their daily activities (fishing, laundry, etc.) have also been shown to have persistence of prevalence and infection. [6] [7] Serology has shown that endemic areas have almost a 100% infection rate, with 60% to 80% of school-age children and 20% to 40% of adults remaining actively infected. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Hookworm -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Hookworm infection is one of the common parasitic infestations in developing countries. They are mainly causing iron deficiency anemia in exposed patients. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of hookworm infections and highlights interprofessional team members' role in collaborating to provide well-coordinated care and enhance patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Schistosomiasis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Schistosomiasis (Schistosoma haematobium), more specifically known as urogenital schistosomiasis, is an endemic disease to many countries in Africa and the Middle East that affects millions of individuals. It causes a range of urogenital issues such as dysuria, hematuria, urinary obstruction, spontaneous abortion, and bladder cancer. This activity outlines the evaluation and management of schistosomiasis (Schistosoma haematobium) and reviews the role of the healthcare team in evaluating and treating patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Schistosomiasis -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Schistosomiasis is a disease that disproportionally affects those with poor hygiene, lack of healthcare resources, and poor living and working conditions overall. Lack of proper education regarding its prevalence and symptoms also impedes recognition and treatment. Population education regarding limiting urination into fresh-water lakes would also be beneficial as there is a short window for miracidia to infect snails.

retrieved_docs.title: Amebic Colitis(Archived) -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Drug treatment cures amebiasis in a few weeks. Intestinal amebiasis can be fatal in developing countries among children, especially those younger than 5 of age. Worldwide, amebiasis is the third most common cause of death due to parasitic infection after malaria and schistosomiasis, with at least 5 deaths per year in the United States. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Schistosomiasis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Urogenital Schistosomiasis is an endemic disease causing a variety of chronic issues in millions of people in Africa and the Middle East. It is easily treatable with praziquantel if there is a high index of suspicion, particularly in endemic areas. The WHO has several easily accessible resources for healthcare providers to utilize in the diagnosis and treatment of UGS and FGS. One such resource is the WHO Pocket Atlas for FGS, available on their website.

retrieved_docs.title: Schistosomiasis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Healthcare providers in non-endemic countries must also remember to consider Schistosomiasis in the evaluation and treatment of individuals presenting with acute or chronic symptoms who have previously traveled to endemic countries.

retrieved_docs.title: Helminthiasis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: In the case of chronic schistosomiasis, intestinal schistosomiasis may present with intermittent abdominal pain, chronic diarrhea, and rectal bleeding. Some people with S. mansoni and S. japonicum infection may develop the hepatosplenic disease with periportal fibrosis, upper abdominal discomfort, palpable nodular, and hard hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, and portal hypertension. Ascites and haematemesis may occur from esophageal varices. The chronic form of the disease may also present as pulmonary hypertension . [11] [19] [28] Urogenital schistosomiasis may present with haematuria, increased urinary frequency, dysuria, and suprapubic discomfort. Urinary tract fibrosis may lead to obstructive uropathy (hydroureter and hydronephrosis), which can cause bacterial superinfection and renal dysfunction. S. hematobium infection also causes squamous-cell carcinoma of the bladder. Female genital schistosomiasis by S. hematobium may present with pain, stress incontinence, infertility, and increased risk of abortion. For men, urogenital schistosomiasis may present with oligospermia, hematospermia, orchitis, and prostatitis. Some non-specific but disabling systemic morbidities are associated with all Schistosoma species, including a pruritic rash that is commonly called ‘swimmers itch, other features include low-grade fever, anemia, and malnutrition with impaired childhood development. Ectopic eggs deposition can lead to very rare but important morbidities. The most common morbidities are spinal compression or encephalopathy. Cerebral schistosomiasis can also occur that may present with symptoms of meningoencephalitis such as headache, vomiting, blurred vision, high-grade fever, and altered sensorium or Jacksonian epilepsy. Spinal cord involvement can present as acute transverse myelitis or subacute myeloradiculopathy, although it is more common in acute schistosomiasis that results in acute lower limb paraplegia or lumbar and leg pain, muscle weakness, sensory loss, and bladder incontinence. [11] [17] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Schistosomiasis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The recommended treatment for schistosomiasis is the anthelminthic praziquantel, dosing regimens vary: 40 mg/kg given as a single dose, or 20 mg/kg every 4-6 hours for three doses

retrieved_docs.title: Schistosomiasis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Healthcare professional and organizational initiation of community or school-wide mass treatment programs in endemic areas would considerably reduce disease burden.  Mass treatment of urogenital Schistosomiasis may also be a cost-effective way to reduce HIV infection in sub-Saharan Africa. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Diphyllobothriasis (Fish Tapeworm Infection) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Praziquantel is a category B medication, as no adequate studies have been conducted in pregnant women. However, accidental administration of praziquantel to pregnant women during mass elimination campaigns has demonstrated no difference in fetal outcomes compared to those that are not treated.[CDC. Parasites-Diphyllobothrium - Resources for Health Professionals. 2020] Safety in children younger than 4 has also not been established, although many children younger than 4 have been treated in mass elimination campaigns for schistosomiasis.[CDC. Parasites-Diphyllobothrium-Resources for Health Professionals. 2020] In individual pregnant or pediatric patients, the risk of no treatment needs to be balanced with the risk of worsening infection with no treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Schistosomiasis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: After treatment with praziquantel, a study of 527 women found no significant reduction in the “sandy patches” seen on colposcopy following treatment. Symptoms to include contact bleeding and vessel abnormalities also remained. [14] Prognosis also depends on the likelihood of reexposure and the need for subsequent treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Schistosomiasis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: In early chronic infection, proper administration of praziquantel can alleviate symptoms and minimize long term inflammation and granuloma formation. However, urogenital lesions are often only partially reversible depending on the degree and type of tissue damage. Sequalae of these lesions can also be chronic.

retrieved_docs.title: Helminthiasis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Helminthiasis causes a significant health problem with increased morbidity and, to some extent, mortality in an underdeveloped and developing country, although it may also occur in developed countries. It remains undiagnosed in many patients, and they suffer a lot due to many complications. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of helminthiasis and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in evaluating and treating patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1590
retrieved_docs.content: Other infections, including parasitic infections, are classified as a subcategory under the broader parent disease group داء الريكتسيات.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 907
retrieved_docs.content: Antinematodal agents constitute a therapeutic class of drugs aimed at eliminating nematode parasites, and within this class the filaricida subgroup is recognized as a specific child category that targets filarial worms. These antinematodal agents are also linked to the management of hlístice, a condition caused by helminthic infection, highlighting their broader role in treating various parasitic worm diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2134
retrieved_docs.content: Antinematodal agents are a class of antiparasitic drugs used to treat infections caused by nematodes, and within this class the filaricida subgroup represents a specialized child category that specifically targets filarial parasites, underscoring the hierarchical relationship in which filaricidal agents are a distinct type of antinematodal medication employed for the management of filarial diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1682
retrieved_docs.content: Adverse effects of medical drugs are recognized as a distinct category of drug-related side effects, and these adverse effects are specifically classified within the context of anthelmintic agents (Anthelminthica). This classification highlights that the side‑effect profile of anthelmintic medications is considered a subset of the broader spectrum of adverse drug reactions, emphasizing the importance of monitoring and managing these effects in patients receiving treatment for parasitic worm infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 126
retrieved_docs.content: as child preparations. Collectively, these inter‑related classifications demonstrate how the imidazole core generates a diverse pharmacological portfolio, guiding clinicians in recognizing structural analogues, potential cross‑reactivity, and alternative therapeutic options across topical, gynecological, systemic antibacterial, antiprotozoal and antiparasitic indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1689
retrieved_docs.content: Arsenic compounds, for amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases, are classified as agents against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1458
retrieved_docs.content: informs preventive strategies, and guides targeted therapeutic interventions. The association of abdominal pain with malignant hypertension exemplifies how severe hypertension may manifest with gastrointestinal discomfort, highlighting the need to consider overlapping pathophysiological and parasitic mechanisms when evaluating the aforementioned conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2232
retrieved_docs.content: Antinematodal agents are a class of antiparasitic drugs used to treat infections caused by nematodes. Kambendazol is a specific medication that belongs to this antinematodal class, exemplifying the type of compound employed to eradicate nematode parasites. Within the broader group of antinematodal agents, there exists a subclass characterized by filaricidal activity—agents that are capable of killing filarial worms. This filaricidal subclass can be considered a “child” category of antinematodal agents, highlighting the specialized use of certain drugs for filarial infections. Recognizing the relationship between the general class (antinematodal agents), its representative member (kambendazol), and the filaricidal subgroup aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy for both nematode and filarial parasitic diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1511
retrieved_docs.content: Antihelmintika, also known as anthelmintics, are a class of medications used to treat infections caused by parasites (paraziti). Levamisol is identified as a specific child drug within the antihelmintika group, representing one of the agents that belong to this class. The relationship between antihelmintika and paraziti underscores that these drugs are employed to eliminate or control parasitic organisms, making the connection between antihelmintika, levamisol, and parasites essential for effective management of helminthic diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2890
retrieved_docs.content: parasitic factors may contribute to or modify these diseases. Moreover, parasitology is specifically linked to nervous system congenital anomalies, indicating a possible etiologic role of parasites in congenital neuro‑developmental disorders. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that the allowed qualifier unifies diagnostic imaging, surgical planning, diagnostic evaluation, and parasitological investigation across a wide spectrum of endocrine, metabolic, renal, ophthalmic, obstetric, cardiovascular, neurological, and infectious conditions, guiding comprehensive patient assessment and interdisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 813
retrieved_docs.content: Antihelmintika are classified as substances categorized by disposition, representing a group of agents used to treat helminth infections. Within this broader class, levamisol is identified as a child (subtype) of antihelmintika, indicating that levamisol is a specific antihelmintic medication. Recognizing levamisol as part of the antihelmintika group helps clinicians understand its pharmacological classification and its role among substances categorized by disposition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 125
retrieved_docs.content: taxonomy links anti‑infective and antiparasitic agents—including antihelmintics, antiprotozoáls, antitrematodals, ectoparasiticides, scabicides, and related pesticides—to their constituent compounds, therapeutic subclasses, formulation details, safety profiles, and interaction warnings, highlighting the interconnected nature of antiparasitic, antimalarial, antischistosomal, trypanocidal, and antifungal pharmacology and the potential for dual‑use drugs in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Benznidazole is a member of the nitroimidazole derivatives that function as antiprotozoal agents against leishmaniasis and trypanosomiasis, indicating its role within this drug class for treating these parasitic infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2540
retrieved_docs.content: planning, diagnostic work‑up, and parasitological evaluation to a wide spectrum of congenital, metabolic, infectious, endocrine, renal, ophthalmic, reproductive, and neurological conditions, thereby supporting comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Kambendazol is classified as an antinematodal agent, indicating that it belongs to the group of medications used to treat infections caused by nematodes and is employed in patient care to eradicate parasitic worm infestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1530
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrofuran derivatives, classified as antiprotozoal drugs, function as therapeutic agents against the protozoal infections leishmaniasis and trypanosomiasis, providing targeted treatment options for these diseases.
